text: In this video, we present a robotic repair of colovascular fistula due to complicated Crohn's disease. The patient was a forty-seven-year-old woman who presented with abdominal pain, vomiting and fever. She reported pneumaturia during the last two weeks. Her past medical history was significant for Crohn's disease, for which the patient was on oral mesalazine. Abdominal CT and MR enterography revealed colovascular fistula with severe narrowing of the corresponding colonic lumen.

In this video, we present a robotic repair of colovascular fistula due to complicated Crohn's disease. The patient was a forty-seven-year-old woman who presented with abdominal pain, vomiting and fever. She reported pneumaturia during the last two weeks. Her past medical history was significant for Crohn's disease, for which the patient was on oral mesalazine. Abdominal CT and MR enterography revealed colovascular fistula with severe narrowing of the corresponding colonic lumen.
The surgeon identifies the interovascular fistula located between the descending colon and the dome of the bladder. The fistula is carefully mobilized circumferentially and then divided.
The surgeon identifies the interovascular fistula located between the descending colon and the dome of the bladder. The fistula is then circumferentially mobilized and divided.

The surgeon retracts the descending colon cranially and incises the medial peritoneal fold. Dissection continues laterally along the Toldt's fascia, with careful identification and protection of the left ureter during the procedure.

The surgeon retracts the descending colon cranially and incises the medial peritoneal fold. Dissection proceeds laterally along the toes fascia, with the left ureter identified and protected during the procedure.
The surgeon progressively divides the sigmoid arteries, followed by the division of the sigmoid pedicles. The proximal rectum is then prepared for further surgical steps.
The surgeon progressively divides the sigmoid arteries, followed by the division of the sigmoid pedicles. The proximal rectum is then prepared for further surgical steps.
The surgeon incises the lateral peritoneal fold and continues the dissection cranially to partially immobilize the splenic flexure.
The surgeon incises the lateral peritoneal fold and continues the dissection cranially to partially mobilize the splenic flexure.
The surgeon divides the proximal rectum using a linear stapler. The bladder is mobilized, and the defect is repaired in two layers using absorbable sutures.
The surgeon uses a linear stapler to divide the proximal rectum, completing the transection with precise alignment.
The surgeon mobilizes the bladder and repairs the defect using absorbable sutures in two layers. Methylene blue is instilled into the bladder to check the effectiveness of the closure.
The surgeon checks the effectiveness of the bladder closure by instilling methylene blue dye into the bladder, observing for any leaks or dye escape.
The uterus is suspended using external traction. The surgical specimen is retrieved through a funnel-shaped incision, and a transcenial anastomosis is performed using a circular stapler.
The uterus is suspended using external traction while the surgical specimen is retrieved through a funnel-shaped incision. A transcenial anastomosis is performed using a circular stapler.

The patient had an uneventful post-operative course and she was discharged on post-operative day five. Final pathology revealed longitudinal fissuring ulcers extending to the serosa, consistent with Crohn's disease. One month after surgery, the patient was doing well and symptom-free.
